subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: 16 role cards total: 1 Doppelganger, 2 Werewolves, 1 Minion, 2 Masons, 1 Seer, 1 Robber, 1 Troublemaker, 1 Drunk, 1 Insomniac, 1 Hunter, 1 Tanner, and 3 Villagers. Also includes 16 role tokens.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Build the role set with exactly 3 more cards than player count. Place one matching role token in the center for every role card in this game so everyone knows which roles are in play. Deal each player 1 role card face-down. Put the 3 extra role cards face-down in the center. Each player secretly looks at their own card.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: There is no first player turn structure in the normal sense because the game is phase-driven. Once everyone has a role and has seen their card, begin the Night phase and resolve wake-ups in exact rulebook order. All role movements happen at night, discussion and accusation happen during day, and elimination is determined by one simultaneous vote.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: 1) Night Phase: roles wake in strict sequence and perform night effects. Wake order is: #1 Doppelganger, #2 Werewolves, #3 Minion, #4 Masons, #5 Seer, #6 Robber, #7 Troublemaker, #8 Drunk, #9 Insomniac, #9a Doppelganger/Insomniac check. Villager, Tanner, and Hunter do not wake. 2) Day Phase: all players discuss, accuse, and claim roles. 3) Vote Phase: everyone points at another player simultaneously. Highest votes die; ties kill all tied players; if all players receive one vote, nobody dies. 4) Win Check: determine winners using final roles and death results.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content: Night role actions: Doppelganger views one other player's card (without swapping) and becomes that role/team, with conditional immediate follow-up: if she sees Seer/Robber/Troublemaker/Drunk she immediately performs that copied action; if she sees Werewolf/Mason she wakes with them later; if she sees Insomniac she checks at #9a; if she sees Minion she joins werewolf team logic. Werewolf: all werewolves open eyes and identify each other; if alone, the other werewolf card(s) are in center (lone wolf option: may view one center card). Minion: wakes after werewolves and sees who they are (werewolves give thumbs up), but werewolves do not learn minion identity. Masons: both must be included if used; they wake and find each other, or infer partner is in center if none seen. Seer: look at one player's card OR two center cards, no movement. Robber: may swap own card with another player's card, then look at the new card; robber adopts new team but does not perform new role's night action. Troublemaker: may swap two other players' cards without looking. Drunk: must swap own card with one center card and does not look at new card. Insomniac: wakes late and looks at own card to see final change state. Day options: players may make truthful or false claims, pressure vote blocs, or withhold info; no player uses additional mechanical powers during day except vote and Hunter pointing consequence if killed.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content: Core twist: starting role and final role can differ because of Robber, Troublemaker, Drunk, and Doppelganger copies. Team membership follows final role for most outcomes, with explicit exceptions in role text (e.g., Robber does not use new role's night action). Minion is werewolf-team aligned but hidden from werewolves. Tanner is an independent objective role: Tanner wants to die and can win even when other teams also win. Hunter creates a chained elimination: if Hunter dies, the pointed-at player also dies regardless of vote totals.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: No VP system is used. The game ends immediately after the single vote resolves deaths and any Hunter chained death. Winning conditions: Village team wins if at least one Werewolf dies. Werewolf team wins if no Werewolves die. If no players are Werewolves (all werewolf cards in center), village wins only if no one dies. Tanner wins only if Tanner dies (independent win condition). Additional interaction from role text: if Tanner dies and no Werewolves die, werewolf team does not win; if Tanner and a Werewolf both die, village team still wins. In ties for most votes, all tied players die. If everyone gets exactly one vote, no one dies.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: Open day discussion by anchoring timeline details, not just claims: what woke, when, and what changed. If you are an information role (Seer/Robber/Insomniac), decide quickly whether early reveal helps create a coherent story web before bluffs harden. If your claim can be cross-checked (e.g., Mason pair, Robber target), name interactions early so contradictions are easier to expose.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content: 1) Treating starting role as permanent. In ONUW, role-swaps mean final role is what usually matters for win checks. 2) Ignoring no-werewolf possibility. If both werewolves are in center, killing someone can throw the village loss condition. 3) Over-trusting unverified lone claims. Good lies often copy role text accurately; demand cross-links and timing consistency. 4) Forgetting Minion incentives. A suspicious non-werewolf may still protect werewolves for team win. 5) Misplaying Hunter consequences by careless pointing and vote piling, causing avoidable double deaths.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content: 1) Information release timing: reveal early to guide table, or hold to catch liars in contradictions. 2) Vote target selection under uncertainty: take a high-confidence werewolf shot vs controlled no-kill lines in possible no-werewolf setups. 3) Claim posture: hard-claiming a role can organize votes, but it also paints a target and may help opposing bluff structures.

metadata.notes: Written from provided official rulebook text including wake order, role actions, vote resolution, and win conditions.
